introSection: Timber Wolf is a wooden roller coaster currently located at Worlds of Fun in Kansas City, Missouri, USA. It was designed by Curtis D. Summers and built by Dinn Corporation.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Timber Wolf opened on April 1, 1989.

Prior to the 2007 season, extensive wood work was carried out on Timber Wolf to make the ride smoother.

On January 4, 2018, Worlds of Fun announced that they would be retracking the ride and adding a 70 degree banked turn at the end, replacing the helix. The work was carried out by Great Coasters International with design and engineering provided by Skyline Attractions.

For the 2019 season, both Prowler and Timber Wolf were retracked.

The ride was retracked once again in 2021. The first drop was reconstructed for the 2023 season.

On March 5, 2025, Worlds of Fun announced that the ride would be closed for the 2025 season. The ride sign was removed and the entrance was fenced off.

In September, the ride sign returned to the attraction entrance. On September 18, the park announced that the ride would reopen on September 20.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: Inside the ride's station, there is a chicken exit for riders who chicken out in line. It is marked by the sign once used by Orient Express, which was demolished in 2003.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Company.

sections.title: Incidents
sections.content:
* On March 31, 1990, 35 people suffered minor injuries when a train collided with a stationary train in front, prior to entering the station. It was found that the control system was unable to handle two trains at the same time. Timber Wolf reopened with a single train in late April while the control system was modified.
 * On June 30, 1995, a 14-year-old girl fell roughly to her death from the Timber Wolf. While her friend riding next to her claims the lap-bar and seat belt both came undone, the park contested this, stating that the all restraints on the train were in place before and after the ride cycle. Two witnesses sitting two rows behind claimed the girl was standing up prior to the incident. In 1998, the mother of the girl reached a $200,000 settlement with Philadelphia Toboggan Company, manufacturer of the train, and Hunt Midwest Entertainment, the former owner of the park.

fullText:
Timber Wolf is a wooden roller coaster currently located at Worlds of Fun in Kansas City, Missouri, USA. It was designed by Curtis D. Summers and built by Dinn Corporation.

Timber Wolf opened on April 1, 1989.

Prior to the 2007 season, extensive wood work was carried out on Timber Wolf to make the ride smoother.

On January 4, 2018, Worlds of Fun announced that they would be retracking the ride and adding a 70 degree banked turn at the end, replacing the helix. The work was carried out by Great Coasters International with design and engineering provided by Skyline Attractions.

For the 2019 season, both Prowler and Timber Wolf were retracked.

The ride was retracked once again in 2021. The first drop was reconstructed for the 2023 season.

On March 5, 2025, Worlds of Fun announced that the ride would be closed for the 2025 season. The ride sign was removed and the entrance was fenced off.

In September, the ride sign returned to the attraction entrance. On September 18, the park announced that the ride would reopen on September 20.

Inside the ride's station, there is a chicken exit for riders who chicken out in line. It is marked by the sign once used by Orient Express, which was demolished in 2003.

The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Company.


 * On March 31, 1990, 35 people suffered minor injuries when a train collided with a stationary train in front, prior to entering the station. It was found that the control system was unable to handle two trains at the same time. Timber Wolf reopened with a single train in late April while the control system was modified.
 * On June 30, 1995, a 14-year-old girl fell roughly to her death from the Timber Wolf. While her friend riding next to her claims the lap-bar and seat belt both came undone, the park contested this, stating that the all restraints on the train were in place before and after the ride cycle. Two witnesses sitting two rows behind claimed the girl was standing up prior to the incident. In 1998, the mother of the girl reached a $200,000 settlement with Philadelphia Toboggan Company, manufacturer of the train, and Hunt Midwest Entertainment, the former owner of the park.
